title: Master AI Video Marketing w/ Magnific CEO Joaquín Cuenca Abela | AI Basics

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Hey everybody, welcome back to our basics series. I get asked all the time as an angel investor, as somebody who helps grow companies, what should I do in terms of customer motion and finding my ideal customer profile? I get asked, hey, how should I do my cap table, legal issues, finance issues? How should I do my revenue recognition? And increasingly, the last two years, how should I use AI to make my startup grow faster, produce better quality products, better, cheaper, faster, you know, that's always the mantra. And you can see all of these great episodes at thisweekinstartups.com/basics.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: We started doing AI basics to help people understand the core things this technology's allowing you to do. And the challenge is, this technology's moving faster and faster, faster than anything we've ever seen. It's easy when we're doing legal because that changes every 5-10 years. Finance, hey, that changes also, like hey, every couple years you might get something out of the IRS to tweak. My Lord, when it comes to AI, it is changing month to month and we've got a great guest today to help us with creative.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: And of course, this AI basic series is brought to you in partnership with my friends over at Google Cloud. Google Cloud really cares about founders, they're constantly giving credits and tools and this very series to help you as a founder figure it out and just figure out how to scale your company and increase your chances of success. You can check out Google's brand new future of AI perspectives on generative media for startups report. You can access that at goo.gle/futureofgenmedia, all one word, no spaces, no dashes, and we'll put that in the show notes and the YouTube description.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Today we are lucky to have Joaquín Cuenca Abela. He is the co-founder and CEO of Magnific, Magnific I think would be the non-French way to say it, formerly known as Freepik. They're based in Spain, I understand. Five billion all-time downloads, over 150 million monthly users for a reason. We're going to talk today about how to use his creative tools, hey, to go help founders be more creative, but then turn their marketing into video, which is a big part of what you're doing, Joaquín, yes?

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Yes Jason, thank you for having me. We are going to talk about how a marketing, a modern marketing department can do more with AI. Many people are focused on how they can do the same things that they were doing before but cheaper and faster. We are going to talk about the void when you do things faster, what can you do that is new.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And marketing is one of those spaces that has data, it's got creative, and then there's a whole bunch of best practices and alchemy between these two, yeah? You have the on one side you've got this great science of marketing and then on the other side...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: You've got the creative and in the middle, dare I say, alchemy. So let's see what you've built and how it works. Maybe you could do a quick demo for our audience of founders here.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Sure, of course. Let me share my screen. Okay, listen, for this demo, I took the liberty to make it a little bit personal for you.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Great.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Okay, I put together like a few restrictions. Okay, I wanted to show what a single person can do in 24 hours, and I'm at that point of making it myself. Okay, I'm not creative. My background is in tech, so there're going to be some glitches in the demo. I hope you will forget me. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: You're pre-forgiven. Yes, it could be some glitches here and there.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Okay. So, the scenario here is I'm going to put together a little ad for your show for this week in startups, okay? And I'm going to put it in post-apocalyptic San Francisco just...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Love it.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Okay. I'm all about the Blade Runner, post-apocalyptic, Walking Dead, zombies. I'm there.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: The first thing that I did is I created a few characters here that I'm going to use on this little show, okay? So this one in particular, he's looking for advice. This comes from a book that I read a while ago about science fiction that is from the 1915, very old book. So I decided to take a little bit this background and mix it with yourself, okay?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Love it.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: So, the first thing is I created a little storyboard for the opening. Okay, so you see how these three characters they're walking through the Golden Gate. So everything is devastated.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Oh, it's like storyboards.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: It's storyboard. Okay, so you can create here like multiple images, you can combine them all together, and then you pick them up and you create a video based on that storyboard. Okay, so you see...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: And now, do you do that with text prompts, or do you just find some images as inspiration and then add text prompts to it?

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Great question. There is a little bit of both. In this case, it was all text prompts, okay? Uh, but in some cases, I like to pick like some images from the internet, use them as a reference, especially when the text is not precise enough. It's very good to use some existing images. But in this case, it was all generated from scratch from some text. Okay.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Great.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: And then I took your logo, This Week in Startups, I made it like rusty and old and all of that, okay?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes. Very dystopian. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Exactly. And then I introduced it in a video and I made the same with you, okay? So I took a few images from yourself and I made it post-apocalyptic, like you're kind of, you know, worn down a little bit.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: No haircut, I got a goatee. Love it.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Yeah, exactly.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: So, you are going to be kind of the guru that those three people seek for advice into how to do like a big project. Again, it's a bit messy because the creative process is messy, but here it shows how you can go from one video to...

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: the next from one image blended all, use audio references. For example I used like this image from the internet as a shotgun that you are having.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And I'm a little dirty, I'm a little greasy, it's been a rough post-apocalyptic era for J-Cal. I'm in my post-apocalyptic era.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Exactly, let's say it's been a rough week, okay? So, we put it all together and at the end I got this little clip here.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: I'm going to play it.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I am excited to see this. Okay, so here we go, the bird screeching, we're going over a rusted-out Golden Gate Bridge.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: There's an old man walking down the bridge, he's out down on his luck. There is no one, everything is desolate.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Oh, there's Fort Mason, you know, the various buildings on the north part of San Francisco. Yeah, here we go.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Just see that the young boy, he's happy because he's still a boy, so there is a little bit of hope in the ambiance.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I mean, this looks amazing. Oh, and there it is, there's the This Week in Startups tattered logo, like a billboard or something.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Exactly. And they get to this whole building...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Maybe my studio? Maybe it's a studio. That's it, okay we're going there, we're going there. Here we go.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Who's that? He's getting his laptop. Oh, he's got a shotgun and his glasses on. This is some dark dystopian stuff here.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It's me, Jason. There he is. Grandpa? I thought you were dead. It's my grandfather.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: The kid needs your help.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I don't do that anymore.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Who knows? I want to build. What do you know about building? What have you ever built? Do you even know how it feels to lose everything?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I want to rebuild civilization.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Wow.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Wow.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Come on, follow me.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: So not bad for one day.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I mean, it's so inspiring and fun, sincerely. Like, I could see, like it makes me want to use this this weekend with my daughters because they are into storytelling and they use CapCut and some of these, you know, like TikTok-type tools, but it doesn't give you this palette. Now, when you were showing all of those storyboards, like sketches and whatnot, and they were getting attached together, did you draw those lines or did the AI draw the lines between the different scenes and collateral and inspiration you gave it?

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: You can do both. Like, as soon as you are typing the prompt, it can suggest, 'Oh, do you want to connect it with this one?' So you start connecting, 'Okay, I want to use this image as a reference, I want to use these voices, I want to use...'

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: ...and you can put it all together super easy. So this thing was done just by me one day, imagine a true creative team like three, four people like to their best. You usually get like somebody that is pretty good at photography, somebody that is good at audio, you put them together and now you can tell much more compelling stories than before.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What's really interesting about what you've built is you're starting to see a movement of people doing launch videos now. Startups are all doing launch videos. Uh, there are some brands uh that are getting a little cheeky on TikTok, Instagram, uh YouTube has the shorts feature now uh where there's reels, I mean all these different short features. You did letterbox there so it looks cinematic, but I assume we could have flipped that and made it in the short feature. And what's great about this is uh this would have been 150,000 dollars to make, you know, maybe 10k a sec— 10k a minute, 20k a minute depending on the level of person you hire. So you do a five minute launch video, could be 50k, could be 100k. Here, you know, what do you charge for this software? What does it cost to do the output? Um, and how do you charge for the product?

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Per second is around 10 cents nowadays. Uh, to be honest, like you usually take like, you do multiple tries to get the right one. So you should assume like there is like one dollar per second. That's nothing. Like the real budget here is going to be your creative team spending their time and doing something great. It's not going to be anymore on the platform.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: The models, um, you're building all these tools, but there are many people building models, open source models. We have frontier models, obviously Google's got all their amazing models that are making great progress, Nano Banana and everything. So what do you use as the back end for this? Is it headless or are you actually building language models as well or image models as well?

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: So we have some image models, like the upscaler is our own technology. We have some skin enhancer that is our own technology, but we use many other like third-party models. We use state-of-the-art obviously, many from Google but also from some other uh platforms. We try to have like state-of-the-art whatever it is in the platform so that it is really easy for the user to achieve the best of the best work that they can do.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Who are the other customers for this product? Obviously, you know, the first person that comes to mind here on This Week in Startups is startups who want to do launch videos, but has this made its way towards uh Hollywood creatives? I would think they would like to use this, uh you know if you were a writer on a television show like The Walking Dead or, you know, any of these hit shows, Better Call Saul. It kind of would be cool to do a little cinematography yourself and, you know, do the dialogue and actually be able to use those actors' voices in the creative process. So the line between a writer and a storyboard artist, a set designer...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: As a designer and a director and a cinematographer, I would assume those lines are blurring, yeah?

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Oh yes. Uh, the great thing about this is that it is raising the, the ceiling and it's also raising the floor, both of them. So you have Hollywood studios, they are using it, it for example like, uh, the company that does uh, the House of David, they have used Freepik to make the production. It's used by all the big studios in Hollywood nowadays and it's used more and more. The growth there is exponential, really many, many users in Hollywood.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: But also many, many marketing departments. So that's the other side of the equation. It's, you get used by companies that are 200, 300 people doing a film, but also by small studios that are 3, 4 people making an ad or making a short film. Okay, it's, it's used in both sides and they are both getting great value out of it.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: The usage is slightly different. I want to clarify, like if you are doing a film, typically you still record actors and you still do like all of that work, but now you can do much more with that footage.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It seems like Hollywood went from just two years ago saying hey, you know, pardon my French, F these tools or like these things are evil, they're bad, to now two years later they're like wait a second. Uh, and I was literally, uh, at this Breakthrough Prize this weekend, it's a science, the Oscars of science, and I was talking to Gal Gadot, she, uh, is very famous for playing Wonder Woman and a bunch of other things.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And I asked her hey, what's your take on all this AI stuff? She says it's incredible. We are now able to make films for one-third the price. We can, you know, film ourselves on a green screen or a grey screen, whatever it happens to be, and then let everybody else do the rest of the work. But we just focus on our performance. We'll be able to make two, three, four, five times as much content and we'll be able to service different audiences. So you know, sometimes people get scared, oh, people are going to lose their jobs, but we're also going to see a Cambrian explosion in creativity where, you know, somebody who's a screenplay writer or they want to be an ad executive, they can't break in, so they just start making spec work. And spec work's got a little bit of a, you know, people are like oh, I don't want to just do spec work, but people write spec scripts all the time. You could make a spec storyboard here, bring it to somebody, and you're just proving your idea out bit by bit, a little bit more, yeah? And it's just so exciting.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, exactly. You can listen, you can take like some sacrifices and put still what looks like a great story, and it's going to be like a great story, uh, for people to see or you can go full on, on a bigger production.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Like I talked to some Hollywood directors and what they told me is that they had a project that it would have never been greenlighted because of the cost to make the project. But now as they managed to reduce the cost, they managed to get that film and employ 600 people doing the film, while otherwise...

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: it would not have been enough budget to do it. So it's actually creating more jobs and I think you're right, like Hollywood is coming to terms with technology because now they see it more and more as an ally rather than something that is going to challenge their jobs or I mean anything.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah. And you've based the company in on the coast of Southern Spain, am I correct?

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: That is right. Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What is the AI talent like? I know it's big in Europe, right? Uh, you have Mistral there, you had a lot of great PhDs etc. But when you pitch people on coming to the south of Spain, incredible seafood, incredible tapas, great lifestyle obviously. What's that like to recruit AI talent and have that as in your in your back pocket?

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Listen, it is not San Francisco, that's for sure. Uh, but at the same time it it offers like a great quality of life. It's also a great place for people to live. So when you pitch, you know, the whole value proposition is you're gonna build something that is exciting, you are going to reshape how creativity works in marketing environments, how films are made, how people tell stories. Uh, and it's in a great place, you know, with a great company. People really buy.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Man, yeah, just amazing and first class. When do you think the line between being able to execute really well on the visuals here, the aesthetic, but then creative storytelling, this seems to be a blocker for some people. You ask any large language model, hey make me a script or make me a storyline. It's good at giving you brainstorming ideas I find, like when I'm writing a blog post or I'm going to be on a podcast I might say, hey I'm going to talk about this, give me some bullet points, some history or some analogies in history, right? I'll ask for things that are analogous or maybe some other people's take on this topic. And it will do a good job brainstorming, but it's not doesn't feel creative. It doesn't feel tight, um, or visionary in any way. It's almost like asking a college student to, you know, give you some ideas and it's good at giving you some ideas and looking something up like a like an intern might or a researcher might, but it's not fully creative. So do you think we're going to hit that point where the creativity of these models starts to match the creativity of humans or do you think it's going to be a tool creative humans use for some time to come?

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: I think it will match the creativity of some humans. What I think is unique to us is that it's our individuality. So in a way, you are you and you are a result of your experiences and what is inside your brain. Uh, me it's it's impossible to replicate you or to replicate me. Okay? We are all very, very like... And AI is very different because it's it's a digital artifact. So you can copy it. Okay, and humans you cannot copy it. So the thing that is important about us is that whatever we do it's unique to us. And I

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: I think that's important. I'm putting that in your work, I think that's important and that's meaningful, okay? So I think that those tools, they are going to drive more opportunities for people that are good at telling stories and are good at putting their own history into something that is meaningful for others. So I think this is going to drive up actually more demand for people that can tell stories, for people that can put together like a good film or a good story.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: The interesting thing for me is as the compute gets better, I could see making a hundred versions of the film you just made in some dynamic way and then using targeting to share them. So we did one dystopian San Francisco, but we could have done Los Angeles, we could have done Sydney, we could have done Galicia or Madrid, you know, any number of cities. And then people from that region could actually experience the same concept, 'Oh my God, everything died in this post-apocalyptic world except the Cybertruck and the Cybertruck made it through with its solar panels and you should buy a Cybertruck to prepare for the apocalypse.' Are people doing that yet where they say, 'Hey, you know, pick the iconic bridge in that city, pick the iconic restaurant or baseball field, the main street, and then make me a hundred versions by city'? Then you could use something like the Google ad network to do hypertargeting, regional targeting, or even other preferences, you know. It could have people who are my age, right? Or it could be more inclusive in different ways. Could have... I could have a Spanish accent, I could have a Japanese... I could speak Japanese. Like, there's so many possibilities here. Have you had customers try that yet?

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Yes, but for simpler things, not yet for video. So what is happening is that for the previous generation of ads, when you have something static, that's something that you can try, that's something that we do. You can localize like 50 different languages, change the food in the ad, change like things like that. And that works pretty well. If you try it with a full video like this one, the problem is that at every step you have a decent probability of error, decent probability of things going wrong. So at the end you take like one minute and it completely loses the track, okay? It diverges. It's not yet... I think we still need, and maybe we'll provide that, we still need kind of a cloud code for this. Something that harness the AI, checks the output and kind of steers it back.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes. This feels like you know we're making progress when we start talking about the harness, the fact-checking, the validation level because what it means is the tools are actually ready for production and we need the fit and the finish and the polish. Let alone doing this in real-time. I mean I'm talking about making a hundred versions but eventually this will be like...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Like Minority Report, where each ad will mention you by name, you know, and it's... they're going to be served dynamically in real-time, you know, and it's... it's going to be pretty crazy, the future.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: But... but I think we're going to get both. We're going to get like some productions that are very, very bespoke to the viewer, but some of... some of them you want the vision of the director. It's like when you go to a restaurant, okay? If they... if they tell you you can get any dish that you want, here you have all the ingredients. That's too much. I want you... I want your opinion on it. So sometimes you want the opinion of the creative to drive the narrative. That's also pretty... pretty tough.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes. All right, listen, Joaquín, thank you so much for educating us. Where can people download the app and find more information?

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Go to magnific.com.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Oh, well, there it is folks, it couldn't be easier than that. And of course, check out Google Cloud's brand new report, Future of AI: Perspectives on Generative Media for Startups. It's a great report that you can get at goo.gle/futureofgenmedia. It's in the description, it's in the show notes. Joaquín, you're famous, you're also featured in the report, yes? They got you in the report.

full_transcript.speaker: Joaquín Cuenca Abela
full_transcript.text: Yes. I want to thank Google for that. Thank you.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes. And all the episodes of Startup Basics from marketing to legal, to accounting, and now AI basics are available at thisweekinstartups.com/basics. This is our gift to the community. Get those basics, lock them in as a founder and just keep building your knowledge and we'll see you next time. Bye bye.

highlights: AI in marketing is shifting from simply doing existing tasks cheaper and faster to enabling creators to fill "the void" with entirely new types of content that were previously impossible to produce.
The barrier to entry for high-end cinematic production has plummeted, allowing a single person to generate professional-grade video for approximately one dollar per second of output.

In Hollywood, AI tools are "raising both the floor and the ceiling" of creativity by allowing projects to be greenlighted that would have historically been rejected due to prohibitive production costs.
While AI can brainstorm and generate digital artifacts, human individuality and personal experience remain unique, non-replicable assets that drive meaningful storytelling.

The next frontier for AI marketing is hyper-localization, where video content can be dynamically adjusted in real-time to feature specific landmarks, languages, or cultural references tailored to a viewer's region.
summary:
This episode of the "AI Basics" series features Joaquín Cuenca Abela, co-founder and CEO of Magnific (formerly Freepik), discussing how generative AI is revolutionizing marketing and creative workflows. Abela demonstrates the power of these tools by showcasing a cinematic, post-apocalyptic marketing video created in just 24 hours—a feat that traditionally would have cost tens of thousands of dollars and required a full production team. The discussion highlights a dramatic shift in content economics, where production costs are dropping from thousands of dollars per minute to roughly one dollar per second, effectively "lowering the floor" for amateurs while "raising the ceiling" for professional creators.

The conversation also explores broader themes regarding the future of creativity and industry adoption. While early skepticism existed, Hollywood and major marketing departments are increasingly viewing AI as an essential ally that allows budget-prohibitive projects to finally get greenlit. Despite the efficiency of these models, Abela emphasizes that human individuality and "creative alchemy" remain irreplaceable; AI acts as a researcher or storyboard assistant, but the unique vision of a director is still what drives a compelling narrative.

Looking forward, the transcript touches on the potential for hyper-personalized, dynamically generated content tailored to specific regions or individual viewer preferences. However, Abela notes that the next major hurdle is creating a "harness" for these models to ensure consistency and quality control in long-form video. Ultimately, the episode underscores that for startups and established brands alike, AI is no longer just a tool for doing things cheaper, but a medium for telling more complex stories that were previously impossible to produce.
description:
The TWiST AI Basics series is made possible by Google Cloud!

Check out Google Cloud’s new report, “Future of AI: Perspectives on generative media for startups,” Featuring thoughts from today’s guest, Joaquín Cuenca Abela
Visit: goo.gle/futureofgenmedia to check it out!

Today’s show:

In today’s edition of TWiST AI Basics, J-Cal is joined by Joaquin Cuenca Abela, co-founder and CEO of Magnific (formerly known as Freepik). We’re discussing how generative AI is transforming the creative landscape for startups.
In watching today’s show you’ll have-consider your expectations of what AI video can actually do for you and your business. What used to take a high end production team months and tens of thousands of dollars now takes 24 hours and one person with patience to learn the right tools. This conversation explores how founders can use these tools to scale their creative output better, cheaper, and faster.

Timestamps:

0:00 Google Cloud - Check out Google Cloud's new report, "Future of AI: Perspectives on generative media for startups," Featuring thoughts from today's guest, Joaquín Cuenca Abela at https://goo.gle/futureofgenmedia
1:10 Welcome to AI Basics, presented by Google Cloud!
2:11 Meet Joaquín Cuenca Abela, CEO of Magnific (formerly Freepik)
3:12 Building a post-apocalyptic SF ad for TWiST in 24 hours
4:25 Storyboarding characters, blending images, and text-prompting scenes
6:39 The finished product: post-apocalyptic Jason!
7:56 How much cheaper is this than traditional advertising?
9:34 The tech stack: Magnific's upscaler and 3rd party frontier models
11:02 Which Hollywood productions are actually using AI?
13:14 Hollywood's 180 from "F these tools" to enthusiastic adoption

youtube_metadata.title: Master AI Video Marketing w/ Magnific CEO Joaquín Cuenca Abela | AI Basics
youtube_metadata.description:
The TWiST AI Basics series is made possible by Google Cloud!

Check out Google Cloud’s new report, “Future of AI: Perspectives on generative media for startups,” Featuring thoughts from today’s guest, Joaquín Cuenca Abela
Visit: goo.gle/futureofgenmedia to check it out!

Today’s show:

In today’s edition of TWiST AI Basics, J-Cal is joined by Joaquin Cuenca Abela, co-founder and CEO of Magnific (formerly known as Freepik). We’re discussing how generative AI is transforming the creative landscape for startups.
In watching today’s show you’ll have-consider your expectations of what AI video can actually do for you and your business. What used to take a high end production team months and tens of thousands of dollars now takes 24 hours and one person with patience to learn the right tools. This conversation explores how founders can use these tools to scale their creative output better, cheaper, and faster.
